items.q: What is the difference between a refugee and an economic migrant?
items.correct: A refugee is forced to flee due to persecution, war or disaster and has legal protections under international law; an economic migrant chooses to move primarily for work or better living standards.
items.misconception: Refugees and economic migrants are basically the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Refugees come from war zones and economic migrants come from rich countries.
items.why: The legal distinction has major consequences — refugees have rights to asylum under the 1951 Convention. Politicians often blur these terms, but they are not interchangeable in policy or geography.

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: What is the Chinese household registration system called?
items.why: It's Hukou. “Shengfen” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which country is the largest recipient of remittances?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: India. “China” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many people make up China's floating population (millions)?
items.why: The real figure is 280. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
